metadata.role: single source of truth for caption generation, caption validation, instruction-dataset construction, fine-tuning, evaluation, and paper
metadata.provenance: Curated from authoritative plant-pathology sources (see reference_registry). Contains no image analysis, no synthetic labels, and no VLM-generated content. Dataset labels are treated as ground truth.
metadata.observability_constraint: Both corpora are single-leaf images (PlantVillage tomato leaflets on plain backgrounds; MangoLeafBD single mango leaves). Captions must describe ONLY leaf-observable features. Fields under 'forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable' encode context features (fruit, twig, flower, whole-tree, yield) that are real for the disease but NOT visible in a leaf image and must never appear in captions.
metadata.pest_vs_pathogen_note: Classes with agent_category in [arthropod_pest, insect_pest] are feeding/mechanical damage, and 'sooty_mould' is a superficial saprophyte on honeydew (leaf tissue not infected). For these, use pest/feeding/surface vocabulary, never infection/lesion vocabulary.
documented_taxonomic_disagreements: Tomato bacterial spot is a four-taxon species complex (X. euvesicatoria pv. euvesicatoria, X. euvesicatoria pv. perforans, X. hortorum pv. gardneri, X. vesicatoria); PlantVillage does not resolve species, so captions stay at disease level.
Tomato leaf mold accepted name unsettled: Fulvia fulva (Videira et al. 2017) with synonyms Passalora fulva and Cladosporium fulvum; some post-2017 work reverts to Cladosporium fulvum.
Mango 'Bacterial Canker' (MangoLeafBD) = the disease usually called bacterial black spot (Xanthomonas citri pv. mangiferaeindicae).
Mango 'Die Back' as a leaf class captures the leaf-level scorch of a twig/branch/tree-decline syndrome (Botryosphaeriaceae, chiefly Lasiodiplodia theobromae).

UCIPM-Tomato.citation: UC Statewide IPM Program (UC ANR). Pest Management Guidelines: Tomato.

UCIPM-Mites.citation: UC IPM. Twospotted spider mite (Pest Notes / guidelines).

UFIFAS-PP351.citation: Pernezny K., Raid R.N., Momol M.T., et al. Target Spot of Tomato in Florida. UF/IFAS EDIS PP351.

UFIFAS-HS1369.citation: Bacterial Black Spot (BBS) of Mango in Florida. UF/IFAS EDIS HS1369.

UMN-LeafMold.citation: University of Minnesota Extension. Tomato leaf mold.

CTAHR-PD46.citation: University of Hawai'i CTAHR. Mango Powdery Mildew. Plant Disease PD-46.

NHB-Mango.citation: National Horticulture Board (India). Mango diseases and their management bulletins.

PHA-GallMidge.citation: Plant Health Australia. Mango leaf gall midge (Procontarinia matteiana) fact sheet.

CABI.citation: CABI Compendium / CABI Digital Library datasheets (mango anthracnose, bacterial black spot, dieback, sooty mould).

CABI-Deporaus.citation: CABI Compendium. Deporaus marginatus (mango leaf-cutting weevil) datasheet.

APS-BSGuide.citation: Jones J.B., Potnis N., et al. Diagnostic Guide for Bacterial Spot of Tomato and Pepper. Plant Health Progress, PHP-11-21-0140-DG (APS).

Constantin2016.citation: Constantin E.C., Cleenwerck I., Maes M., et al. (2016). Taxonomic revision within the Xanthomonas bacterial-spot complex. Plant Pathology / Syst. Appl. Microbiol.

Timilsina2019.citation: Timilsina S., et al. (2019). Taxonomy and genetic structure of tomato-pepper bacterial-spot xanthomonads (X. euvesicatoria, X. hortorum pv. gardneri, X. vesicatoria). Frontiers in Microbiology / Phytopathology.

Videira2017.citation: Videira S.I.R., Groenewald J.Z., Nakashima C., et al. (2017). Mycosphaerellaceae - Chaos or clarity? Studies in Mycology 87:257-421 (adopts Fulvia fulva for tomato leaf mold).

Sossah2024.citation: Sossah F.L., et al. (2024). A critical review on bacterial black spot of mango caused by Xanthomonas citri pv. mangiferaeindicae. Forest Pathology 54:e12860.

Arauz2000.citation: Arauz L.F. (2000). Mango anthracnose: economic impact and current options for integrated management. Plant Disease 84(6):600-611.

Chomnunti2011.citation: Chomnunti P., et al. (2011). Sooty moulds (Capnodiaceae and allies): biology of superficial honeydew-associated fungi. Fungal Diversity.

MangoLeafBD.citation: Ahmed S.I., Ibrahim M., Nadim M., et al. (2023). MangoLeafBD: A comprehensive image dataset to classify diseased and healthy mango leaves. Data in Brief 47:108941.

Agrios2005.citation: Agrios G.N. (2005). Plant Pathology, 5th ed. Elsevier Academic Press.

APS-CompTomato.citation: Jones J.B., Zitter T.A., Momol M.T., Miller S.A. (eds.) (2014). Compendium of Tomato Diseases and Pests, 2nd ed. APS Press.

Ploetz2003.citation: Ploetz R.C. (ed.) (2003). Diseases of Tropical Fruit Crops. CABI Publishing (mango chapter).

primary_symptoms: uniform green intact pinnately compound leaf
serrated/lobed leaflet margins
prominent midrib and pinnate venation
slight surface pubescence (trichomes)
severity_indicators: absence of any lesion, discoloration, curling, webbing, coating, or cutting
key_differentiating_features: complete absence of pathological signs
uniform coloration
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: any disease or pest sign (spots, lesions, yellowing, mottling, webbing, coating, galls, cutting)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: a healthy tomato leaflet with uniform green coloration and intact margins

diseases.scientific_name: Xanthomonas spp. (four-taxon complex: X. euvesicatoria pv. euvesicatoria, X. euvesicatoria pv. perforans, X. hortorum pv. gardneri, X. vesicatoria)
diseases.taxonomy_note: Species complex, repeatedly revised; PlantVillage label does not resolve species, so captions should stay at disease level and not name a species.
diseases.disease_progression: Seed/transplant-borne inoculum, then small water-soaked leaf spots, then dark angular necrotic lesions, then coalescence, blighting and defoliation.
primary_symptoms: numerous small dark-brown to black greasy/water-soaked spots
angular (often vein-delimited)
frequently ringed by a yellow (chlorotic) halo
secondary_symptoms: shot-hole / tattered look as centers dry and drop
leaf_margin_changes: marginal necrosis/scorch when lesions coalesce at margins
leaf_curling: minimal; slight marginal distortion when severe
texture_changes: slightly sunken to flat, greasy/water-soaked early lesions
severity_indicators: extent of marginal scorch and defoliation

mild: a few tiny water-soaked dark specks with faint halos
moderate: many dark angular spots with distinct yellow halos

key_differentiating_features: many small angular dark greasy spots WITH yellow halos
no concentric rings
no pycnidia
shot-hole
recommended_caption_vocabulary: small dark angular spots with yellow halos scattered across the leaflet
management_practices: avoid overhead irrigation and handling wet plants
treatment_recommendations: copper-based bactericides (often + mancozeb), preventive; efficacy limited by copper-tolerant strains

diseases.taxonomy_note: Tomato-specialized Alternaria (A. linariae / A. tomatophila) sometimes distinguished from A. solani.
diseases.disease_progression: Oldest/lowest leaves first, circular lesions enlarge with concentric rings, leaflet chlorosis, upward defoliation (also stem collar rot and fruit, not leaf-visible).
primary_symptoms: brown-to-dark-brown circular lesions with concentric rings (target/bull's-eye)
often surrounded by a yellow halo
begin on oldest/lowest leaves
lesion_morphology: sometimes dark velvety sporulation in center
leaf_margin_changes: not margin-specific; entire leaflet can senesce
leaf_curling: affected leaflets distort as they senesce
necrosis: prominent dark necrotic zones with rings
chlorosis: pronounced yellow halos and general leaflet yellowing

mild: one or a few small dark spots on lower leaves, faint rings
moderate: enlarging target-pattern lesions with yellow halos

key_differentiating_features: coarse concentric bull's-eye rings + yellow halo + lower-leaf-first distribution
diagnostic_visual_features: target/bull's-eye concentric rings with yellow halo on older leaves
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: white underside sporulation (that is late blight)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: brown circular lesions with concentric rings and a yellow halo on a lower leaflet
management_practices: remove/destroy lower infected leaves and residue

diseases.taxonomy_note: Oomycete (water mold), NOT a true fungus; family Peronosporaceae.
environmental_conditions: explosive epidemics under cool wet conditions
diseases.disease_progression: Rapid: water-soaked leaf lesions, then large greasy blotches, then white underside sporulation, then whole-leaf and whole-plant collapse within days; petioles/stems affected.
primary_symptoms: large irregular water-soaked/greasy grayish-green to brown-black blotches
often starting at leaf tips/margins
white downy sporulation on underside at lesion margins under humidity
lesion_morphology: white fuzzy sporulation ring on lower surface
severity_indicators: presence and extent of underside sporulation

mild: small water-soaked pale-green spots, often at margins/tips

key_differentiating_features: water-soaked grayish-green blotches
no rings
white downy sporulation on underside
rapid spread
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: fruit rot (firm greasy brown fruit lesions)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: large irregular water-soaked grayish-green blotches with a pale margin
treatment_recommendations: protectant (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) + oomycete-specific (mefenoxam, cymoxanil, phosphonates, mandipropamid)

diseases.taxonomy_note: Accepted name unsettled; Videira et al. 2017 adopt Fulvia fulva; some post-2017 work reverts to Cladosporium fulvum.
diseases.disease_progression: Lower/older leaves first: pale diffuse upper patches with olive-to-brown velvety mold on underside; leaflets yellow, curl, wither, drop.
primary_symptoms: diffuse pale-green to yellow patches on the upper (adaxial) surface with indistinct margins
olive-green to grayish-brown velvety/felty fungal growth on the underside (abaxial) - key sign
secondary_symptoms: leaflets yellow, curl, wither, and drop
leaf_margin_changes: leaflets curl and dry from margins in advanced stages
leaf_curling: upward/inward curling and wilting of affected leaflets
necrosis: browning and death of leaflets late
texture_changes: velvety/felty olive-to-brown coating on the underside (key diagnostic texture)

mild: pale-green diffuse spots on the upper surface
moderate: yellow upper patches + olive velvety mold on the underside

key_differentiating_features: olive-green/brown velvety sporulation on the LOWER surface beneath diffuse yellow upper patches
greenhouse setting
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: white powder on the upper surface (that is powdery mildew)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: diffuse yellow patches on the upper surface with olive-green velvety mold beneath
treatment_recommendations: protectant fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb) and labeled options for protected culture

environmental_conditions: survives on residue and weed hosts
diseases.disease_progression: Lower/older leaves first: numerous small circular spots with dark margins and tan/gray centers, black pycnidia in centers, leaflet chlorosis, upward defoliation.
primary_symptoms: numerous small (~1.5-3 mm) circular spots with dark-brown margins and light-gray-to-tan centers
tiny black pycnidia visible as pinpoint dots in centers (key diagnostic)
secondary_symptoms: general yellowing of heavily spotted leaflets
lesion_morphology: circular, dark-margined, gray/tan center with pycnidia
diseases.lesion_size: small, ~1.5-3 mm (typically smaller than early blight)
leaf_margin_changes: not margin-specific; whole leaflet yellows/dies when spots dense
texture_changes: dry; visible pinpoint black pycnidia in centers

mild: a few small circular dark-margined spots on lower leaves
moderate: many circular gray-centered spots with black pycnidia

key_differentiating_features: many small circular spots + gray/tan centers + pinpoint black pycnidia
no concentric rings
diagnostic_visual_features: small circular gray-centered spots with visible black pycnidia
recommended_caption_vocabulary: numerous small circular spots with dark margins, gray centers, and tiny black pycnidia
management_practices: remove crop residue and weed hosts

diseases.taxonomy_note: Arthropod pest (mite), NOT a pathogen. The class represents feeding injury. Use pest/feeding-injury vocabulary, never infection/lesion/pathogen.
diseases.host_plant: Very wide host range, including tomato
environmental_conditions: high temperature (>27 C) and low humidity
outbreaks often follow broad-spectrum insecticide use
diseases.disease_progression: Fine stippling, then chlorotic/bronzed patches, then underside webbing, then desiccation/bronzing and leaf drop.
primary_symptoms: fine pale/yellow stippling (tiny chlorotic flecks) on the upper surface from cell-content feeding
fine silk webbing on the underside
mites and eggs may be visible as tiny dots
leaf_color: stippled yellow to bronzed/tan to gray-brown
lesion_morphology: NOT lesions - diffuse stippling and bronzing
no defined margins, rings, or pycnidia
leaf_margin_changes: curling and desiccation of margins when severe
necrosis: bronzing/desiccation rather than discrete necrotic lesions
texture_changes: fine silk webbing on the underside (key sign)

mild: faint fine stippling on the upper surface

key_differentiating_features: fine stippling + silk webbing (underside) + tiny moving mites
no lesions, rings, or pycnidia
diagnostic_visual_features: fine stippling and webbing (and mites, if visible)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: fine yellow stippling and bronzing with fine webbing on the underside
management_practices: conserve natural enemies (avoid broad-spectrum insecticides that flare mites)
treatment_recommendations: biological control with predatory mites (Phytoseiulus persimilis, Neoseiulus spp.)

environmental_conditions: important in Florida and humid tropics
diseases.disease_progression: Leaf spots, then lesions with subtle concentric rings and pale centers, then coalescence and blighting (also stem and sunken fruit lesions, not leaf-visible).
primary_symptoms: brown-to-dark-brown lesions with subtle concentric rings and light-brown/gray centers
sometimes a diffuse yellow (chlorotic) halo
often pinpoint dark flecks early
diseases.lesion_size: pinpoint early to enlarging moderate; variable

moderate: enlarging lesions with subtle concentric rings + pale centers + yellow halo

confused_with: early blight (coarser rings, lower-leaf-first, larger halos)
key_differentiating_features: subtle fine concentric rings + pale center + distribution throughout the canopy
no pycnidia
diagnostic_visual_features: fine concentric-ring target lesions with pale centers
recommended_caption_vocabulary: brown lesions with subtle concentric rings and pale centers scattered across the leaflet
treatment_recommendations: fungicides (chlorothalonil, mancozeb, strobilurins) with resistance-management caution
prevention_recommendations: no resistant commercial cultivars available; emphasize cultural control and preventive spraying

scientific_name_synonyms: closely related to Tobacco mosaic virus (TMV)
diseases.taxonomy_note: Positive-sense ssRNA virus; family Virgaviridae, genus Tobamovirus. Expresses as systemic mottling/distortion, NOT discrete lesions.
diseases.host_plant: Tomato, tobacco, pepper, and many others
environmental_conditions: spread mechanically (handling, tools, contaminated seed, grafting)
extremely stable and persistent on surfaces/debris
diseases.disease_progression: Systemic: light/dark-green mosaic mottling, leaf distortion (fern-leaf), stunting; expression varies with strain, temperature, and light.
primary_symptoms: mottled light- and dark-green mosaic pattern on leaflets
leaf distortion - narrowing, puckering, fern-leaf/thread-leaf
surface blistering (rugosity)
lesion_morphology: no discrete lesions/spots - a systemic mottle
lesion_distribution: systemic; whole-leaflet, most evident on new growth
necrosis: generally none on leaves (certain strains/conditions can cause streak/necrosis)

mild: mild light/dark-green mottling on young leaves

confused_with: TYLCV (but TYLCV is cupping + marginal yellowing, not mosaic)
key_differentiating_features: light/dark-green mosaic mottling + fern-leaf distortion, without spots/lesions
systemic
recommended_caption_vocabulary: a light-and-dark-green mosaic mottling with fern-leaf distortion of the leaflets
prevention_recommendations: resistant cultivars (Tm-1, Tm-2, Tm-2 squared genes)

diseases.taxonomy_note: Single-stranded DNA virus; family Geminiviridae, genus Begomovirus. Whitefly-vectored. Expresses as cupping + yellowing + stunting, NOT lesions.
environmental_conditions: transmitted by whitefly Bemisia tabaci (persistent, circulative)
favored by warm conditions and high whitefly populations
diseases.disease_progression: Systemic: new growth shows upward cupping/curling, marginal/interveinal yellowing, reduced leaflet size, stunting; flower drop.
primary_symptoms: upward curling/cupping of leaflets
interveinal and marginal chlorosis (yellowing)
marked reduction in leaflet size
overall stunting with a bushy appearance
most pronounced on new/apical growth
leaf_color: yellowing, especially leaf margins and interveinal areas of younger leaves
diseases.lesion_size: not applicable (reduced overall leaflet size is the sign)
lesion_distribution: systemic; most pronounced on new/apical growth

mild: slight upward curling and marginal yellowing of the youngest leaves

confused_with: physiological leaf roll (no yellowing/stunting, older leaves)
key_differentiating_features: upward cupping + marginal/interveinal yellowing + reduced leaflet size + stunting on new growth
diagnostic_visual_features: small, upward-cupped, yellowing leaflets on new growth
recommended_caption_vocabulary: small upward-cupped leaflets with yellowing margins on new growth
management_practices: whitefly management (insecticides, reflective mulches, insect screens/exclusion)
treatment_recommendations: no chemical cure (viral); manage the vector

diseases.taxonomy_note: No pathogen or pest. Young flush is normally coppery-red to pale/light-green (healthy, not disease).
primary_symptoms: uniform green lanceolate to oblong-lanceolate simple leaf with an entire (smooth) margin
prominent midrib and pinnate venation
leathery (coriaceous), glossy texture
young flush normally coppery-red to pale/light-green
leaf_curling: none (young flush may be limp before hardening - normal)
severity_indicators: absence of spots, coatings, galls, cutting, or discoloration
key_differentiating_features: no spots, galls, coatings, cutting, or necrosis
uniform lamina
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: any disease or pest sign (spots, lesions, galls, coating, cutting, mold, mottling)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: a healthy mango leaf with uniform green coloration, a glossy surface, and an intact margin

diseases.taxonomy_note: A species complex; C. gloeosporioides is the classic name, with additional cryptic species regionally.
environmental_conditions: rain, dew, and splashing spread conidia
most important field/postharvest disease in humid tropics
diseases.disease_progression: Young flush most susceptible: small dark spots, coalescence into irregular necrotic blotches, shot-hole, distortion; also blossom blight and postharvest fruit rot (not leaf-visible).
primary_symptoms: small dark-brown to black, irregular necrotic spots
frequently along leaf margins and tips and between veins
enlarge and coalesce into large angular/irregular dark necrotic areas
young leaves distort
lesion_morphology: no macroscopically visible pycnidia (acervuli are micro-scale)
diseases.lesion_size: ~1-5 mm early to large coalesced blotches
lesion_distribution: margins, tips, and scattered across the lamina
leaf_margin_changes: marginal necrosis/blackening (black patches along the leaf margin) - key leaf sign, consistent with MangoLeafBD

mild: tiny dark specks/spots on young leaves

confused_with: bacterial canker/black spot (angular, water-soaked, raised, vein-limited, chlorotic-halo)
key_differentiating_features: irregular dark-brown/black necrotic blotches (especially margins/tips) + shot-hole
lesions dry/necrotic, NOT raised water-soaked angular (that is bacterial)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: irregular dark-brown to black necrotic blotches along the leaf margin, with shot-holing
management_practices: orchard sanitation and pruning for airflow

diseases.common_name: Bacterial black spot / bacterial canker of mango
diseases.taxonomy_note: MangoLeafBD label 'Bacterial Canker' is the same disease usually called bacterial black spot in the literature.
environmental_conditions: entry through wounds (wind/sand abrasion) and stomata
spread by rain splash and wind
diseases.disease_progression: Leaf lesions begin water-soaked, then raised, angular, black, vein-delimited lesions (often with a chlorotic halo), then older lesions dry to light-brown/ash-gray; also twig cankers and star-shaped gummy fruit lesions (not leaf-visible).
primary_symptoms: raised, angular, black leaf lesions delimited by veins
initially water-soaked
frequently surrounded by a chlorotic (yellow) halo
lesion_morphology: raised, angular, vein-limited, black, water-soaked to necrotic
chlorosis: yellow halo around lesions - key sign

mild: tiny water-soaked angular spots with a faint halo
moderate: raised black angular lesions with distinct chlorotic halos

confused_with: anthracnose (irregular, dry, not raised/water-soaked, not strictly vein-angular)
key_differentiating_features: raised, angular (vein-limited), black, water-soaked lesions with a yellow halo (vs anthracnose's flat, dry, irregular necrosis)
diagnostic_visual_features: raised angular black vein-limited lesions + chlorotic halo
recommended_caption_vocabulary: raised angular black lesions with yellow halos, delimited by the leaf veins

diseases.taxonomy_note: Insect pest (beetle), NOT a pathogen. The class represents mechanical cutting/feeding damage. Use cutting/feeding vocabulary, never lesion/spot/infection.
diseases.host_plant: Mango (especially nursery seedlings and young flush)
diseases.disease_progression: Adults make scissor-like cuts on young leaves; cut/notched/trimmed margins and windowpane feeding scars; cut leaf tips drop; larvae feed between leaf surfaces.
primary_symptoms: clean, scissor-like cuts and notches at leaf margins/tips
windowpane translucent feeding scars (epidermis left intact) on young leaves
overall trimmed/ragged leaf appearance
leaf_color: green lamina with brown/dry cut edges
lesion_morphology: NOT lesions - mechanical cuts, notches, and windowpane scars
lesion_shape: linear/clean cuts, notched margins, irregular missing tissue
diseases.lesion_size: from small marginal notches to large missing sections
lesion_distribution: margins and tips of young/tender leaves
leaf_margin_changes: cut, notched, trimmed - key sign (MangoLeafBD: leaves look cleanly cut)
texture_changes: cut edges and translucent windowpane areas

mild: small marginal notches / windowpane scars on flush

key_differentiating_features: clean scissor-like cuts + windowpane scars on tender flush
no spots, mold, galls, or mosaic
recommended_caption_vocabulary: cleanly cut and notched leaf margins with translucent windowpane feeding scars
management_practices: collect and destroy fallen cut leaves
treatment_recommendations: insecticide sprays on new flush during adult activity

diseases.taxonomy_note: Botryosphaeriaceae complex (chiefly Lasiodiplodia theobromae). As a MangoLeafBD leaf class it captures the leaf-level scorch of a twig/branch/tree-decline syndrome; captions describe only the leaf.
environmental_conditions: opportunistic on stressed trees (drought, heat, wounds, pruning cuts)
diseases.disease_progression: Twig/branch dieback from the tip downward; leaves on affected shoots yellow, brown, curl, and dry; defoliation; vascular discoloration in stems (not leaf-visible).
primary_symptoms: yellowing followed by browning from the leaf tip/margin inward
leaves on affected shoots wilt, curl, dry, and become necrotic (scorched)
leaf drop
lesion_morphology: diffuse marginal/tip necrosis (scorch) progressing inward, rather than discrete spotted lesions
diseases.lesion_size: large diffuse areas up to whole-leaf
leaf_margin_changes: browning/scorching from the margins and tip - key leaf sign
leaf_curling: curling and drying of affected leaves

mild: yellowing/browning of leaf tips/margins on a shoot

confused_with: anthracnose (discrete dark spots vs diffuse scorch)
key_differentiating_features: diffuse marginal/tip scorch + curling/drying associated with shoot dieback (vs discrete spotting)
no pycnidia typically visible on the leaf
diagnostic_visual_features: marginal/tip browning, scorch, curling, and drying (whole-leaf decline)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: leaf browning and scorching from the tip and margins inward, with curling and drying
management_practices: prune and destroy affected shoots well below symptoms

diseases.taxonomy_note: Insect pest (fly), NOT a pathogen. The class represents larval-induced galls. Use gall/bump vocabulary, never flat lesion/spot.
diseases.disease_progression: Adult lays eggs in tender leaves; larvae feed inside; small raised wart-like galls form within ~a week; galls turn necrotic; premature leaf drop.
primary_symptoms: numerous small, raised, wart-like/pimple-like galls (bumps) on the lamina
initial tiny reddish spots
galls hard and discrete, often with a central larval puncture
leaf_color: sometimes a chlorotic ring around each gall
lesion_morphology: raised discrete galls (swellings) - not flat lesions
lesion_distribution: densely scattered across the lamina, often between veins
leaf_curling: leaf distortion when galls are dense
texture_changes: raised, bumpy, wart-like - key sign (MangoLeafBD: pimple-like/raised wart-like galls)

mild: small reddish spots / tiny bumps on new leaves
moderate: numerous raised green-to-brown galls (pimples) across the lamina

key_differentiating_features: raised discrete wart/pimple galls (3-D bumps) with a central puncture (vs flat lesions)
no mold or ooze
diagnostic_visual_features: raised pimple/wart-like galls scattered on the lamina
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: flat necrotic lesions as the primary feature
recommended_caption_vocabulary: numerous small raised wart-like galls scattered across the leaf lamina
management_practices: collect and destroy fallen galled leaves
treatment_recommendations: insecticide sprays timed to adult activity/egg-laying on new flush

diseases.taxonomy_note: Obligate biotroph, ectophytic; anamorph name Oidium mangiferae commonly used; teleomorph assignments vary in the literature.
environmental_conditions: high humidity/dew during flowering and flush
a dry-weather disease (unlike anthracnose, not rain-driven)
diseases.disease_progression: White superficial powdery growth on young leaves, inflorescences, and young fruit; leaf distortion; purplish-brown weathering; flower/fruit drop.
primary_symptoms: white-to-grayish superficial powdery fungal coating (mycelium + conidia) on the leaf surface (especially young leaves and undersides)
patches coalesce
secondary_symptoms: grey necrotic lesions on the upper surface
purplish-brown weathering where powder wears away
leaf_color: white powdery patches over the surface
lesion_morphology: superficial powdery mycelial coating - initially wipeable
diseases.lesion_size: variable; coalesce to cover large areas
leaf_curling: young leaves curl (often downward) and distort
texture_changes: white powdery/mealy coating - key diagnostic texture; superficial

mild: small white powdery patches on young leaves

confused_with: sooty mould (BLACK, not white; older leaves; on honeydew)
key_differentiating_features: WHITE superficial powdery coating on young tissue, wipeable (vs black sooty mould)
diagnostic_visual_features: white powdery/mealy coating on the leaf surface
forbidden_symptoms_not_leaf_observable: black surface coating (that is sooty mould)
recommended_caption_vocabulary: a white powdery coating on the leaf surface with young-leaf distortion
treatment_recommendations: strobilurins - timed to flowering and new flush

diseases.scientific_name: Capnodium spp. (e.g., C. mangiferae, C. ramosum) and allied fungi (Meliola, Tripospermum, Cladosporium)
diseases.taxonomy_note: Superficial saprophyte/epiphyte growing on insect honeydew; the leaf tissue underneath is NOT infected. Use surface-coating vocabulary; never assert tissue necrosis.
diseases.pathogen_type: Fungus (superficial saprophyte/epiphyte, not a tissue pathogen)
diseases.host_plant: Mango (surface only; indirect - follows sucking-insect infestation)
environmental_conditions: presence of honeydew-secreting insects (hoppers, scales, mealybugs, aphids)
diseases.disease_progression: Honeydew deposits on the leaf; black superficial fungal colonization; black sooty coating over the (usually upper) leaf surface; shading reduces photosynthesis; the film flakes/peels when dry, revealing green tissue beneath.
primary_symptoms: black-to-dark-brown superficial sooty/velvety coating on the leaf surface (typically adaxial)
superficial (sits on the surface) and wipeable/peelable
forms a film or crust
secondary_symptoms: when dry, the film flakes to reveal green, undamaged tissue beneath
lesion_morphology: superficial black film/crust - NOT tissue lesions
lesion_distribution: upper surface, along the midrib and where honeydew falls
texture_changes: sooty, velvety, powdery-black, crusty film - key sign; wipeable/flaky
severity_indicators: coating coverage and thickness (not tissue damage, which is absent)

mild: thin black smudges/patches on the upper surface
moderate: continuous black sooty coating over part of the leaf
severe: entire leaf surface black-coated and dull

key_differentiating_features: BLACK superficial wipeable coating with GREEN, healthy tissue underneath
associated with honeydew/sucking insects (vs white powdery mildew; vs anthracnose's embedded necrosis)
diagnostic_visual_features: black sooty superficial coating (wipeable) over green tissue
recommended_caption_vocabulary: a superficial black sooty coating over the leaf surface, with the tissue green beneath
management_practices: control honeydew-producing insects (scales, mealybugs, hoppers, aphids) - the root cause
treatment_recommendations: insecticides/horticultural oils for the insect vectors; the mould clears once honeydew stops
